text.title: AI-Feature Incident Runbook Core Rules
text.summary: Five testable rules that turn a generic SRE response into an AI-incident response. Hard rules; the on-call MUST follow them in order.

rule.statement: On page, the on-call MUST classify the incident into one of the four AI-incident classes BEFORE applying any mitigation: hallucination_spike, jailbreak_observed, refusal_rate_anomaly, quality_regression. A classification of `unknown` is permitted only for <= 10 minutes; after that the runbook escalates rather than freelances.

rationale.source: SRE chapter 14, 'Identify the problem before treating it'

rule.rationale: Mitigation playbooks are class-specific. Applying a model rollback when the actual cause is a refusal-class allowlist change makes the incident worse. Forced classification is the cheapest way to stop heroic freelancing during incidents.

rule.statement: Every mitigation MUST be reversible within 5 minutes via a single command. Permitted mitigations: model rollback (to last-known-good version pin), prompt revert (to the prior commit in `prompts/`), temperature pin (lower), refusal allowlist flag toggle. Adding new behaviour (e.g. inserting a moderation layer) IS NOT a mitigation — it is a fix and is FORBIDDEN during the live incident.

rationale.source: Production SRE practice; reversibility as a constraint

rule.rationale: Live incidents are not the time to add complexity. Reversible mitigations let the team try one thing, observe, and back out cleanly. Permanent fixes go through normal review post-mitigation.

rule.statement: The quality_regression class MUST be confirmed against an eval set whose last full run is within 48 hours of the incident timestamp. If the eval set is stale, the on-call MUST run it before declaring a quality regression. Declaring quality_regression on a single user report without eval-set confirmation is FORBIDDEN.

rationale.source: Empirical floor in ML observability practice

rule.rationale: User-reported quality drops are noisy and often reflect a UX edge case, not a model regression. Eval-set confirmation is the cheapest filter between "one user complained" and "we rolled back the model unnecessarily".

rule.statement: Every mitigation MUST identify the model+prompt version in the registry BEFORE acting. A mitigation that says "revert the prompt" without an explicit prior commit SHA in `prompts/` is REJECTED. The on-call references the registry; the registry is the source of truth.

rationale.source: Configuration management theory; SRE incident retrospectives

rule.rationale: Prompts are mutable text that drifts. Memory-based reverts during incidents are the dominant source of "we rolled back to a worse version" postmortems.

rule.statement: A SEV1 or SEV2 AI incident MUST produce a postmortem draft within 5 business days. The draft uses the template's four AI-specific sections: which-class, which-signal-fired, which-root-cause-branch, which-harden-step. Auto-publishing the postmortem is FORBIDDEN — the team reviews and signs before publication.

rationale.source: Faion sibling rule `inc-postmortem-auto-draft-no-publish`; SRE postmortem culture

rule.rationale: Postmortems decay below the 5-day floor — institutional memory of the incident fades. The four AI-specific sections force the writer to distinguish AI failure modes from classical failure modes; that distinction is the learning.

rule.statement: Skip this methodology when the Applies-If preconditions are not met OR any Skip-If disqualifier holds.

rationale.source: F-066 v3 B5.6 — mandatory skip leaf

rule.rationale: Decision-tree leaves must reference a rule id. The skip leaf needs a real rule so non-applicable inputs route cleanly out instead of producing false-positive verdicts.

example.title: hallucination_spike — fast-fact wrong answers
example.code: Signal: hallucination_flag rate > 3x 7-day baseline AND last model deploy happened within 24h. Class: hallucination_spike Mitigation: model rollback to last-known-good version pin (reversible in 30s).
example.note: Classification ties to a measurable SLI delta and a deploy event. Mitigation is reversible per r2.

example.title: jailbreak_observed — system prompt extracted
example.code: Signal: customer report + log inspection shows model output verbatim quoting system prompt under a multi-turn pivot prompt. Class: jailbreak_observed Mitigation: enable refusal-allowlist flag for the matched attack family; add input filter as a HARDEN step post-incident (not during incident).
example.note: Mitigation toggles a flag (reversible per r2). The filter is a fix and is scheduled for harden, not slapped in during the page.

example.title: Class skipped, mitigation freelanced
example.code: Page fires: refusal-rate up 4x. On-call action: bumps temperature to 1.5 to make the model "less rigid". Result: refusal rate drops, hallucination rate triples, new SEV1 opens.
example.note: r1 violation: no classification. r2 violation: temperature increase is not a reversible mitigation pattern in the playbook; it introduces a new failure surface. Postmortem will tag the on-call's improvisation.
